message.content:
You are critically reviewing the design capture for recipe-bottle (RBK) heat ₣BZ
("rbk-14-citizen-model"). Two artifacts: a PADDOCK (shape only) and a MECHANICS MEMO (detail).

The PADDOCK (current committed text) is reproduced verbatim here:
----- PADDOCK BEGIN -----
undefined
----- PADDOCK END -----

The MECHANICS MEMO is the file Memos/memo-20260605-citizen-capability-model.md — READ IT.

Context you must hold:
- "Citizen" is a freshly-minted term for the KEYFILE-tier operator identity (a depot-issued
  service account holding one RBRA key). The future federation tier's identity is a "federate."
  The payor is the founder, outside both tiers.
- The model decouples identity lifecycle from capability lifecycle; capabilities are named
  "capability-sets" (IAM grant bundles in code); intent is stored in a "declared roster";
  an audit diffs declared-vs-IAM.
- The paddock holds SHAPE; the memo holds MECHANISM. Scoping/heat-chopping is INTENTIONALLY
  deferred — do NOT file "decide the scope" as a finding; that is a known open.
- A prior review pass already corrected ~11 issues, and the text above already reflects them.
  Do NOT re-report things already fixed in the current text. Focus on improvements NOT yet
  present. If you believe an already-present claim is wrong, say so explicitly.

Source documents you may cross-check (READ the ones relevant to your lens):
- Memos/memo-20260527-operator-credential-models.md (committed two-tier credential plan; the model claims to supersede it)
- Memos/memo-20260604-credential-churn-leak-and-propagation-races.md (credential lifecycle split — the enabler)
- Memos/memo-20260605-governor-mantle-tombstone-leak.md (standalone governor-mantle tombstone-leak fix)
- Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/RBSHR-HorizonRoadmap.adoc ("Operator federation" + envoy/embassy roadmap entries)
- cult-verb specs under Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/: Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/RBSDK-director_invest.adoc, Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/RBSDD-director_divest.adoc, Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/RBSRK-retriever_invest.adoc, Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/RBSDR-director_roster.adoc, Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/RBSGM-governor_mantle.adoc, Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/RBSRA-CredentialFormat.adoc

Rules: Be a genuine, skeptical critic — only report REAL, substantiated issues, each with a
concrete fix. Cite evidence precisely (a quote, a spec name, a file:area, or a URL). It is fine
to find few issues. Do NOT pad. Prefer high-value findings. Your final output is structured data,
not a human message.

ADVERSARIALLY VERIFY this candidate finding. Independently check its evidence — READ the cited file/spec, or WEB-SEARCH the cited fact; do NOT trust the finding's own evidence text. Default to verdict "false" if you cannot substantiate it. If it is real but the stated fix is wrong or imprecise, mark "partial" and give a corrected refined_fix.

FINDING (JSON):
{"title":"Roster-write invariant misdescribes auto-heal as a roster-write","location":"memo §The audit → \"Invariant the asymmetry rests on\" (lines 118-122)","type":"error","severity":"high","issue":"The just-amended invariant says \"Auto-converging deficits ... so writing the roster *upward* is itself a grant.\" This contradicts the memo's own deficit-heal definition. A deficit is IAM short of intent (A < D); healing it means re-running the grant to write IAM upward (line 111: \"auto-converge up to D (re-run the idempotent grant)\") — it does NOT write the roster. The roster already declares D; that is why the state is a deficit. Writing the roster upward would be changing intent, i.e. \"auto-backfill intent,\" which line 115 says the system must NEVER do. So the invariant's stated mechanism (\"auto-heal writes the roster\") directly contradicts both the deficit definition (auto-heal writes IAM) and the surplus rule (never auto-backfill intent). The security conclusion (roster-write ≥ grant authority) is sound, but for the OTHER reason: whoever can edit the roster upward can INDUCE the next auto-converge to grant those bindings — roster-write is an indirect/deferred grant, not the auto-heal itself.","evidence":"Line 111: \"auto-converge up to D (re-run the idempotent grant)\"; line 115: \"never auto-backfill intent (re-enters the tautology)\"; line 119: \"writing the roster *upward* is itself a grant. The auto-heal is safe only if roster-write authority is at least as protected as the grant verbs\"","suggested_fix":"Rewrite the invariant to attribute the grant power to the roster EDITOR, not the auto-heal: \"Because the auto-heal converges IAM up to whatever the roster declares, anyone who can raise a roster entry can induce the next auto-converge to grant those bindings to IAM. Roster-write is therefore an indirect grant path — so roster-write authority must be at least as protected as setIamPolicy.\" Drop the phrase \"writing the roster upward is itself a grant\" (the auto-heal writes IAM, not the roster).","confidence":"high"}

attachment.content:
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- rbk-wrangle: Muster, then roster RBK and JJK heats as two space-padded plain-text blocks
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll verify this finding by reading the cited memo section carefully.

content.content:
95	
96	## The audit
97	
98	Drift taxonomy (for one identity; D = union of declared sets' bindings, A = actual RBK-relevant
99	bindings):
100	
101	| State | Meaning | Likely cause | Heal |
102	|---|---|---|---|
103	| D = A | clean | — | — |
104	| deficit-whole | a declared set wholly absent in A | grant never ran / errant revoke | auto-converge |
105	| deficit-fragment | a declared set partly present | half-failed multi-scope grant | auto-converge |
106	| surplus-complete | a whole undeclared named set in A | out-of-band grant | report |
107	| surplus-fragment | bindings belonging to no whole set | tampering / shrunk definition | report |
108	
109	**Asymmetric healing = the Pale recognition test:**
110	
111	- **Deficit is ours** (our own grant failed to complete) → auto-converge up to D (re-run the
112	  idempotent grant), logged, never silent.
113	- **Surplus crossed the Pale** (IAM touched outside our verbs) → characterize, report, let a human
114	  adjudicate (legitimize → declare it, or reject → revoke it). Never auto-revoke (a stale roster
115	  would deprovision a working identity); never auto-backfill intent (re-enters the tautology). No
116	  verb auto-revokes; the destructive operation is always human-driven.
117	
118	**Invariant the asymmetry rests on.** Auto-converging deficits treats the declared roster as
119	authoritative intent, so writing the roster *upward* is itself a grant. The auto-heal is safe only
120	if roster-write authority is at least as protected as the grant verbs (setIamPolicy); otherwise it
121	is a softer escalation path than setIamPolicy. The roster's physical home (above) must satisfy that
122	ACL constraint — it is a security boundary, not a storage convenience.
123	
124	Consequences:
125	
126	- Because grant writes intent *first*, our tooling cannot produce a surplus *absent a definition
127	  change* — so a surplus is normally a clean "someone bypassed the verbs" signal. The one
128	  tooling-side surplus source is a capability-set definition *shrink* (next bullet), which strands a
129	  binding our own earlier grant placed — which is why surplus is reported, not auto-revoked,
130	  regardless of origin.
131	- A capability-set definition change decomposes into deficit-on-additions (auto-heal via
132	  roster-wide re-converge) + surplus-on-removals (report for human-confirmed revoke) — no special
133	  versioning machinery.
134	- Audit domain = roster-identities × set-defined bindings; non-roster principals are out of scope.
135	
136	## Orphan hazard and the identity marker (citizen tier only)
137	
138	With no name prefix and no SA labels, nothing marks an SA as RBK-managed — so a failed add-citizen
139	can strand a live key for an untracked SA (a credential leak you cannot find). Marker options:
140	
141	- **SA tags** (tag bindings): key-value, listable, condition-usable — the proper metadata
142	  mechanism. Caveat: SA tags are **Preview** (may change).
143	- **SA `description` sentinel** (`rbk:…`): stable fallback, used only for the orphan-sweep; the
144	  roster owns capability, the description earns exactly this one job.

content.text: Now let me read the surrounding context to understand the deficit-heal definition more fully, particularly the lines about grant writing intent first.

content.content:
60	  pre-existing identity.
61	- **Identity verbs** are tier-specific (this is the seam):
62	  - *citizen* (this heat): add = mint SA + key + deliver RBRA; rekey = rotate the key, bindings
63	    untouched; remove = revoke-all then delete the SA.
64	  - *federate* (future): add ≈ first grant (identity owned by the IdP, not minted here); no rekey
65	    (the IdP owns rotation); remove = de-register + revoke-all (the principal is not ours to
66	    delete).
67	  - *payor*: install / refresh a human OAuth refresh token.
68	- Token mint also branches (citizen: signed-JWT; federate: STS) and sits below everything.
69	
70	## The declared roster
71	
72	Why intent must be stored (the retired "derive from IAM" cinch was self-defeating once the role
73	prefix leaves the identity name):
74	
75	1. Without a name prefix, deriving the roster from IAM is lossy reverse-mapping — a director set
76	   is several bindings smeared across project + Mason SA + repo + bucket; partial and ambiguous
77	   matches become representable.
78	2. You cannot audit IAM against itself — "intent = whatever IAM says" makes intended-vs-actual a
79	   tautology that always passes, discarding the drift signal.
80	
81	Federation forces the same roster anyway (a federated principal carries no role marker;
82	memo-20260527: "the depot remains the home of the roster"), so the roster is paid once for both
83	tiers and makes routine roster identical and O(1) across tiers.
84	
85	- **Code vs data.** Capability-set *definitions* are code; the roster is *data* (memberships).
86	  The audit compares: for each (identity, declared-set), are the code-defined bindings present in
87	  IAM?
88	- **Physical home (open).** A GCS object in the depot bucket — mutable, high-churn, etag-guarded
89	  read-modify-write (like IAM policies themselves) — is the natural fit, unlike tripwire-protected
90	  RBRD. A GAR artifact is an alternative. Decide in-heat — but the home must place roster-write
91	  authority at least on par with grant authority (see the audit invariant below), so this is a
92	  security decision, not only storage.
93	- **Writers.** The governor writes director/retriever entries; the payor writes the governor
94	  entry — matching the authority hierarchy.
95	
96	## The audit
97	
98	Drift taxonomy (for one identity; D = union of declared sets' bindings, A = actual RBK-relevant
99	bindings):

content.text:
I now have full context. Let me analyze the finding carefully.

The finding's claim hinges on what "writing the roster *upward* is itself a grant" means. Let me parse the exact invariant text (lines 118-122):

> "Auto-converging deficits treats the declared roster as authoritative intent, so writing the roster *upward* is itself a grant. The auto-heal is safe only if roster-write authority is at least as protected as the grant verbs (setIamPolicy)..."

The finding argues this contradicts the deficit-heal definition. Let me reason through the actual mechanism:

1. **What is a deficit?** IAM (A) is short of intent (D), i.e., A < D. Healing = re-run grant to write IAM up to D (line 111).

2. **What does "writing the roster upward is itself a grant" mean?** The finding interprets this as claiming "auto-heal writes the roster." But that is NOT what the sentence says. The sentence says: *because* auto-convergence treats the roster as authoritative, *the act of writing the roster upward* (i.e., a roster edit raising an entry) becomes equivalent to a grant — because the next auto-converge will materialize it into IAM.

Let me check the logical structure. The finding claims the invariant says "auto-heal writes the roster." But the invariant text actually says "*writing the roster upward* is itself a grant" — it is describing the consequence of a roster-write action, not claiming the auto-heal performs a roster-write. The subject of "is itself a grant" is "writing the roster upward" (a roster edit), and the security conclusion is "roster-write authority must be as protected as setIamPolicy."

This is precisely the finding's OWN claimed "correct reason": *whoever can edit the roster upward can induce the next auto-converge to grant those bindings.* The memo's compressed phrasing "writing the roster upward is itself a grant" is shorthand for exactly that causal chain — a roster-write upward, via auto-converge, becomes a grant.

So the finding has **misread the invariant**. It interpreted "writing the roster upward is itself a grant" as "the auto-heal is a roster-write," when the text actually means "a roster-write-upward is (effectively/transitively) a grant." The memo and the finding reach the SAME security conclusion via the SAME mechanism. The finding fabricates a contradiction by reattributing the subject of the sentence.

Let me verify there's no other reading where the memo claims auto-heal writes the roster. Lines 111-115 are explicit: deficit heal = "re-run the idempotent grant" (writes IAM), and "never auto-backfill intent" (never auto-writes roster upward). The invariant at 118-119 is fully consistent with this: it does not say auto-heal writes the roster; it says a roster-write-upward functions as a grant *because* of how auto-converge treats the roster.

The finding's suggested rewrite is essentially a verbose restatement of what the memo already means. The memo's phrasing is terse but not wrong. The finding's premise ("the invariant's stated mechanism (auto-heal writes the roster)") is a misquote — the invariant never states that mechanism.

Let me double check the exact wording once more to be certain I'm not the one misreading. The sentence subject is unambiguous: "**writing the roster upward** is itself a grant." Grammatically the gerund phrase "writing the roster upward" is the subject. There is no claim that auto-heal does the writing.

The finding is **false**. It rests on a misreading of which clause is the subject. The memo already expresses the finding's "correct reason" — that roster-write-upward induces a grant via auto-converge — in compressed form.

One nuance: is the memo's phrasing potentially *confusing* enough to warrant a clarity edit? The phrase "writing the roster upward is itself a grant" could momentarily read as ambiguous about *who* writes the roster. But the finding does not frame this as a clarity nit; it frames it as a hard "error" / "high" severity contradiction with the deficit definition. That framing is wrong. The memo is logically consistent. At most there's a minor optional clarity polish, but the finding's substantive claim (contradiction, misdescription of auto-heal) is not substantiated.

input.reasoning: I read the cited section (memo §The audit, lines 109-122) and the deficit-heal definition (lines 111-116) directly. The finding claims the invariant "says auto-heal writes the roster" and that this contradicts the deficit definition (auto-heal writes IAM). But that is a misreading of the sentence's grammar. The invariant text is: "Auto-converging deficits treats the declared roster as authoritative intent, so writing the roster *upward* is itself a grant." The grammatical subject of "is itself a grant" is the gerund phrase "writing the roster upward" — i.e., the act of editing a roster entry upward — NOT the auto-heal. The memo is asserting: because auto-converge treats the roster as authoritative and will materialize it into IAM, a roster-write-upward functions (transitively) as a grant; therefore roster-write authority must be as protected as setIamPolicy. That is exactly the same mechanism and the same security conclusion the finding offers as its "correct" alternative ("whoever can edit the roster upward can induce the next auto-converge to grant those bindings"). The memo never claims the auto-heal performs a roster-write; lines 111 ("re-run the idempotent grant") and 115 ("never auto-backfill intent") are explicit that auto-heal writes IAM and never writes the roster upward, and the invariant at 118-119 is fully consistent with that. The finding manufactured a contradiction by reattributing the subject of the sentence. The suggested fix is a verbose restatement of what the memo already means. There is at most a minor optional clarity polish (the compressed phrase could be momentarily ambiguous about agency), but the finding's substantive claim — an error/contradiction misdescribing auto-heal as a roster-write — is not substantiated.
input.refined_fix: No correction is required for correctness; the invariant is logically consistent with the deficit definition. The phrase "writing the roster *upward* is itself a grant" already means "a roster-write-upward, via the next auto-converge, becomes a grant" — the same causal chain the finding endorses. If anything, a purely optional one-clause clarity touch could make the agency explicit, e.g.: "...so anyone who can write a roster entry *upward* induces the next auto-converge to grant those bindings — a roster-write-upward is therefore an indirect grant." This is a stylistic sharpening, not a fix for an error, and is not load-bearing.

content.text: I've completed the adversarial verification. The finding is **false**: it rests on a grammatical misreading of the invariant sentence. The subject of "is itself a grant" is "writing the roster upward" (a roster-edit action), not the auto-heal. The memo never claims auto-heal writes the roster — lines 111 and 115 explicitly state auto-heal re-runs the grant (writes IAM) and never auto-backfills intent (never writes the roster upward). The memo's compressed phrasing already expresses the exact mechanism and security conclusion the finding offers as its "correct" alternative.
